column4: mechanism
NSAID inhibits renal prostaglandins, blunting the antihypertensive effect of ACE inhibitors and raising the risk of reduced renal function, especially with volume depletion.
Combined anticoagulant and antiplatelet effect substantially increases bleeding risk, particularly gastrointestinal bleeding.
NSAID displaces warfarin from protein binding and impairs platelet function, increasing bleeding risk on top of anticoagulation.
ACE inhibitor plus potassium-sparing diuretic raises serum potassium; risk of clinically significant hyperkalemia.
Clarithromycin strongly inhibits CYP3A4, raising simvastatin plasma levels and the risk of myopathy or rhabdomyolysis.
Amlodipine (Norvasc) is a moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor; combined use increases simvastatin exposure. FDA labeling recommends limiting simvastatin to 20mg/day with amlodipine.
Both increase serotonergic activity; combination carries a risk of serotonin syndrome.
Amiodarone inhibits warfarin metabolism via CYP2C9, increasing INR and bleeding risk; dose reduction and closer monitoring are typically required.
Amiodarone reduces digoxin clearance, raising digoxin levels and toxicity risk; dose reduction is usually needed.
Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19-mediated activation of clopidogrel, potentially reducing its antiplatelet effect.
Ciprofloxacin inhibits warfarin metabolism, increasing INR and bleeding risk.
NSAIDs reduce renal clearance of methotrexate, raising the risk of methotrexate toxicity, especially at higher methotrexate doses.
ACE inhibitors reduce renal lithium clearance, raising lithium levels and toxicity risk.
NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance, raising lithium levels; monitor lithium levels if the combination is unavoidable.
Combined beta-blocker and non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker can cause severe bradycardia, AV block, or hypotension.
Macrolides can potentiate warfarin's anticoagulant effect; monitor INR more closely when co-prescribed.
Calcium carbonate binds levothyroxine in the gut, reducing absorption; separate dosing by several hours.
Both are potent vasodilators; combined use can cause severe, potentially life-threatening hypotension. Contraindicated.
Tramadol has been associated with increased INR when combined with warfarin; monitor coagulation status.
NSAIDs blunt the diuretic and antihypertensive effect of spironolactone and, combined with its potassium-sparing action, raise hyperkalemia risk.
Cimetidine reduces renal clearance of metformin, modestly raising metformin levels.
Clarithromycin's CYP3A4 inhibition raises atorvastatin levels, increasing myopathy or rhabdomyolysis risk.
ARB plus potassium-sparing diuretic raises serum potassium; risk of clinically significant hyperkalemia.
Thiazide diuretics reduce renal lithium clearance, raising lithium levels and toxicity risk.
Both increase serotonergic activity; combination carries a risk of serotonin syndrome.
Combined beta-blocker and non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker can cause severe bradycardia or AV block.
Verapamil reduces digoxin clearance and adds additive AV-nodal blockade, raising digoxin toxicity and bradyarrhythmia risk.
Omeprazole can modestly increase warfarin exposure via CYP2C19 interaction; clinically significant effect is uncommon but INR monitoring is reasonable.
Gemfibrozil markedly raises simvastatin plasma levels via glucuronidation/OATP1B1 inhibition, substantially increasing rhabdomyolysis risk. Combination is generally avoided.
Fluconazole strongly inhibits CYP2C9, markedly increasing warfarin levels and INR; bleeding risk is significantly elevated.
